mensHealth.tipSleepLow: Less than 7h of sleep reduces testosterone by up to 15%. Prioritize 8h.
mensHealth.tipStressHigh: High cortisol directly suppresses testosterone. Ashwagandha + meditation.
mensHealth.tipDriveLow: Low libido is an early sign of sub-optimal testosterone or elevated prolactin.
mensHealth.tipRecoveryLow: Poor recovery raises cortisol and impairs testosterone synthesis. Check sleep + zinc.
mensHealth.tipFocusLow: Low mental clarity is linked to sub-optimal testosterone and high cortisol.
mensHealth.tipActivity: Resistance training 3–4x/week is most effective for testosterone based on evidence.
mensHealth.phaseOffDesc: Natural hormone production recovering. Focus on health markers and natural optimization.
mensHealth.phaseBlastDesc: Peak anabolic phase. Maximize training stimulus, monitor blood pressure and organ stress markers.
mensHealth.phaseCruiseDesc: Maintenance dose. Bridge between blasts. Monitor haematocrit and lipids regularly.
mensHealth.phasePCTDesc: Post-cycle therapy. HPTA restart in progress. Prioritize recovery, sleep and health markers.
mensHealth.quizHeroSub: 4 questions to build your protocol — from natural testosterone optimization to advanced athlete management. All data is private.
mensHealth.articleDisclaimer: Educational content, not medical advice. Always consult a doctor for personalized guidance.
mensHealth.vitalitySub: Evaluate each pillar honestly. Your T-Opt Score updates in real-time.
mensHealth.pelvicSub: Improves erectile function, urinary control, sexual performance, and core stability.
mensHealth.pelvicInstructions: Squeeze 5s → Relax 5s → Repeat. 10–15 cycles. Daily practice gives results in 4–6 weeks.
mensHealth.unlockStackSub: Upgrade to Pro to see personalized recommendations based on your goals.
mensHealth.suppDisclaimer: Always consult a doctor before starting supplements, especially if taking medication or on cycle.
mensHealth.healthMarkersSub: Log your latest lab values and blood pressure readings. Track trends over time.
mensHealth.peakDay7Body: Deplete glycogen. Carbs: 1.5g/kg. Moderate sodium. Normal water intake.
mensHealth.peakDay6Body: Low carb (1g/kg). High protein. Start monitoring daily weight fluctuations.
mensHealth.peakDay5Body: Note muscle fullness and vascularity. Increase carbs if flat.
mensHealth.peakDay4Body: Carbs: 4–6g/kg. Low GI sources: sweet potato, rice, oats. Keep fat low.
mensHealth.peakDay3Body: Carbs: 6–8g/kg. Watch for spill-over (blurred definition). Reduce if needed.
mensHealth.peakDay2Body: Reduce water slightly (not eliminate). Increase potassium foods. Last sodium manipulation.
mensHealth.peakDay1Body: Sip water. Carb load backstage (rice cakes + honey). Pump up 20 min before stage. Trust your prep.
mensHealth.emptyTrack: Log your vitality daily to see your trend here.
mensHealth.peakWeekSub: Carb load day-by-day, water manipulation and stage prep
mensHealth.annualPanelBody: At {{age}} years old, tracking total testosterone, free testosterone, SHBG, oestradiol and thyroid annually gives you the data to proactively optimize.
mensHealth.fatLossBody: Aggressive calorie restriction below 15 kcal/kg/day suppresses testosterone. Aim for 15–20% max deficit. Prioritize protein 2.2g/kg to preserve muscle.
mensHealth.progressiveBody: Add 2.5kg to compound lifts weekly, or increase reps before increasing load. Train each muscle group 2x/week for hypertrophy. Deload every 4–6 weeks.
mensHealth.unlockDeepSub: AI hormone analysis, training periodization recommendations and enhanced athlete protocols.

art1.title: The Testosterone Blueprint
art1.body: Testosterone peaks at 6–8am and is lowest at night. Sleep is the single biggest lever — one week of 5h sleep reduces testosterone to castrate levels in young men. The protocol: 8h sleep, resistance training 4x/week, zinc 30mg, vitamin D3 5000IU, and cortisol management. These four levers alone can raise total testosterone 20–30% in deficient men.

art2.title: Sleep & Testosterone: The Hard Science
art2.body: REM sleep is when 95% of daily testosterone is produced. Every hour below 8 reduces output proportionally. Alcohol at night suppresses GH release by 70%. The magnesium-sleep-testosterone triangle: magnesium deficiency impairs sleep quality, which impairs testosterone, which reduces magnesium uptake. Fix all three simultaneously.

art3.title: Training for Maximum Testosterone Response
art3.body: Compound lifts (squat, deadlift, bench, row) with high intensity (75–90% 1RM) produce the greatest testosterone response. Keep sessions under 60 minutes — cortisol rises sharply after. Rest 90–120 seconds between sets for hormonal response. Overtraining suppresses testosterone; under-recovery does too. Periodise: 3 weeks hard, 1 week deload.

art4.title: Eating for Testosterone
art4.body: Fat is the raw material of testosterone — dropping below 20% dietary fat crashes testosterone. Cholesterol from eggs and red meat is not the enemy; it's the precursor. Zinc from red meat and oysters, selenium from Brazil nuts (2/day), and saturated fat from whole food sources are the dietary cornerstones. Avoid chronic calorie restriction — it's the fastest way to suppress hormonal output.

art5.title: Cortisol: Testosterone's Arch Enemy
art5.body: Cortisol and testosterone are directly antagonistic. Every cortisol spike suppresses Leydig cell function and reduces testosterone synthesis. The most effective cortisol management tools: consistent sleep schedule, ashwagandha KSM-66 (600mg), B-complex supplementation, removing chronic stressors, and limiting caffeine after 12pm.

art6.title: Harm Reduction for PED Users
art6.body: The greatest risk for enhanced athletes is cardiovascular: left ventricular hypertrophy, elevated haematocrit, and dyslipidaemia. Non-negotiables: blood tests every 8 weeks (lipids, CBC, liver enzymes, hormones, PSA over 40), cardiovascular exercise 4x/week, TUDCA + NAC for organ protection, BP monitoring, and minimum 1:1 cycle-to-off ratio. Never blast without a health baseline.

art7.title: Peak Week: The Science of Coming In Dry
art7.body: Peak week for bodybuilding is about glycogen supercompensation, not extreme dehydration. Protocol: 4 days moderate carb depletion, 3 days carb load (600–800g/day), water manipulation (not elimination) in final 24 hours. Sodium manipulation: reduce 2 days out, not eliminate. Potassium-rich foods (banana, avocado) in final days improve definition. Diuretics: only with medical supervision.

art8.title: Which Blood Tests Every Man Should Track
art8.body: Baseline panel: Total Testosterone, Free Testosterone, SHBG, LH, FSH, Oestradiol, DHEA-S, Prolactin, CBC, CMP (liver/kidney), lipid panel (LDL, HDL, triglycerides), Vitamin D, ferritin, HbA1c. Enhanced users add: haematocrit, PSA (over 40), thyroid panel (TSH, T3, T4). Test in the morning (7–9am) in a fasted state for accurate baseline.

bp.label: Blood Pressure
bp.tip: Check weekly on cycle. Hawthorn berry + celery seed if elevated.

hct.label: Haematocrit
hct.tip: Elevated HCT = blood clot risk. Donate blood or reduce compound dose.

ldl.label: LDL Cholesterol
ldl.tip: AAS crush HDL and raise LDL. Niacin + fish oil + cardio are your tools.

liver.label: ALT (Liver)
liver.tip: Elevated ALT = hepatic stress. TUDCA + NAC mandatory. Stop orals if > 3x normal.

psa.label: PSA (40+)
psa.tip: AAS can accelerate prostate growth. Annual PSA test for all enhanced users over 40.

enhanced.bloodworkBody: Enhanced athletes must monitor lipids, liver enzymes, haematocrit, and hormones every 8 weeks minimum. These values change rapidly with AAS use. Get bloods before, during and after each cycle.
enhanced.cardioBody: The #1 cause of premature death in enhanced athletes is cardiovascular disease from LV hypertrophy, elevated haematocrit and dyslipidaemia. 4x/week cardio (zone 2) is non-negotiable, not optional.

tips.focus_1: Start with your hardest task before checking email.
tips.focus_2: Enable Focus Mode for a 25-min distraction-free sprint.
tips.focus_3: Write your single most important task here before anything else.
tips.overload_1: Eat the frog: identify the ONE task that moves the needle most.
tips.overload_2: Batch similar tasks — all calls together, all writing together.
tips.overload_3: Say no to one thing today. Put it in Notes, not your to-do list.
tips.procrastination_1: Start with a 2-minute version of the scary task.
tips.procrastination_2: Pair the task with music or a reward after completion.
tips.procrastination_3: Tell a partner your commitment — accountability works.
tips.balance_1: Block personal time as non-negotiable calendar events.
tips.balance_2: Log your evening wind-down in Wellness → Sleep tracker.
tips.balance_3: Set a hard stop time today and honour it.

profile.title: Profile

phaseDesc.menstrual: The uterine lining sheds. Oestrogen and progesterone are at their lowest.
phaseDesc.follicular: FSH rises, follicles develop, oestrogen climbs. Energy and clarity increase.
phaseDesc.ovulation: LH surge triggers egg release. Oestrogen peaks. You feel your best.
phaseDesc.lutealEarly: Progesterone rises with corpus luteum formation. Body temperature elevates.
phaseDesc.lutealLate: PMS window. Progesterone drops if no fertilisation. Cortisol sensitivity rises.

phaseNutrition.menstrual: Iron-rich foods: lentils, leafy greens, red meat
phaseNutrition.follicular: Complex carbs, fermented foods, lean protein
phaseNutrition.lutealEarly: Magnesium (dark chocolate, nuts), B6 for mood
phaseNutrition.lutealLate: Reduce salt + sugar. Tryptophan foods (turkey, eggs) for serotonin.

phaseTip.menstrual: Use a heat pad, cut caffeine and prioritise sleep.
phaseTip.follicular: Schedule demanding projects and creative work now — your brain is in turbo mode.
phaseTip.ovulation: Your voice is literally more attractive now — lean into social situations.
phaseTip.lutealEarly: This is the best phase for detailed admin work and deep focus.
phaseTip.lutealLate: Reduce alcohol and processed foods. Track your PMS symptoms to identify patterns.

insight.pmsBody: Magnesium glycinate (300mg) taken before bed has strong clinical evidence for reducing cramps, bloating and mood changes.
insight.stressLutealBody: Your stress level combined with the PMS window can amplify cortisol and worsen symptoms. Prioritise a cortisol-reduction practice today.
insight.fertilityBody: You're in your peak fertility window. If you're actively trying to conceive, this is the optimal time. If not, stay aware.
insight.estrogenBody: Serotonin and dopamine are elevated in this phase. A great window to schedule difficult conversations, presentations or creative projects.
insight.sleepBody: Under 7 hours of sleep raises cortisol, suppresses progesterone and can cause cycle irregularities. You are currently below the minimum.
insight.lowCarbBody: Very low carb diets can suppress LH and FSH, causing irregular or absent periods. If your cycle is irregular, consider a carb-cycling approach.
insight.painBody: Severe menstrual pain is not normal and may indicate endometriosis, fibroids or adenomyosis. We strongly recommend speaking to a doctor or gynaecologist.
insight.pillBody: The combined pill can deplete B6, B12, folate, magnesium and zinc. Consider a B-complex supplement and regular testing.
insight.athleticBody: High-intensity training can suppress oestrogen and disrupt your cycle (RED-S). Monitor your period — any disruption is a warning signal from your body.
insight.periBody: Hormonal fluctuations often begin in the late 30s, even with regular periods. Track mood, sleep and cycle changes closely.
insight.calorieBody: Aggressive caloric deficits suppress leptin and thyroid hormones, potentially stalling weight loss and disrupting cycles. Sync training intensity with your cycle.

magnesium.name: Magnesium Glycinate
magnesium.reason: Reduces cramps, PMS mood symptoms and improves sleep

iron.name: Iron + Vitamin C
iron.reason: Replenish iron lost during menstruation; C enhances absorption

omega3.name: Omega-3 (EPA/DHA)
omega3.reason: Anti-inflammatory; reduces prostaglandin-driven cramps. As effective as ibuprofen in trials

bComplex.name: B-Complex (B6, B12, Folate)
bComplex.reason: Oral contraceptives deplete B vitamins — critical for mood and methylation

zinc.name: Zinc
zinc.reason: Contraceptive pill depletes zinc, affecting immunity and skin

ashwagandha.name: Ashwagandha KSM-66
ashwagandha.reason: Clinically proven adaptogen for cortisol reduction and hormonal balance

magLThreonate.name: Magnesium L-Threonate
magLThreonate.reason: Crosses blood-brain barrier; improves deep sleep and reduces night anxiety
